slides.text: All offensive players are asked to be on the perimeter. Ideally, two players are behind the net.

SPIN - Players are asked to aggresively move the ball around the perimeter of the offense. This requires SUPPORTING teammates by running to them when they need help, and by anticipating the need to SUPPORT two or three passes in advance
slides.subtitle: SPIN & SHARE

slides.text: After players cut and/or filter through the middle, they are asked to not park themselves in the crease. This creates spacing issues and places defenders in areas that we are trying to access!
This asks players to adjust and adapt to where their teammates are so they don't end up stacked on top of eachother unecessarily.

TWIST -- > one of four basic motions that we want out players to leave this offense with a sense of; we need to help solve the perennial youth lacrosse problem of off ball players being in the way of ball carriers.

A twist is also known in lacrosse as a "shallow clear through"; a player removes himself (and his defender) out of the space that the dodger is trying to attack. In this diagram, imagine X1 is trying to dodge into the space that X2 and D2 are occupying.
slides.subtitle: Twist

slides.text: If X2 doesn't find a way to move effectively, D2 is able to effectively double team the ball without forfeiting his ability to cover X2.
slides.subtitle: Twist

slides.text: So, X2 can "TWIST" underneath X1 and bring his defender with him.

DRIFT -- > one of four basic motions that we want out players to leave this offense with a sense of; we need to help solve the perennial youth lacrosse problem of off ball players being in the way of ball carriers.
slides.subtitle: DRIFT

slides.text: Also sometimes called a "fade" - a drift occurs when the off ball offensive player moves away from the ball carrier.
slides.subtitle: DRIFT

slides.text: This motion puts the off ball defender (D3) in a "decision position" and does not allow him to cover two people at once!
slides.subtitle: DRIFT

slides.text: Importantly, other players need to make sure that they are reacting to this motion and removing themselvs and their defenders from help positions
slides.subtitle: DRIFT

slides.text: PICK -- > one of four basic motions that we want out players to leave this offense with a sense of; we need to help solve the perennial youth lacrosse problem of off ball players being in the way of ball carriers.
slides.subtitle: PICK

slides.text: Pick play is impossible to capture in a static diagram. As a starting principle -> it is important for young players to set picks that send their teammates to dangerous areas of the field. Ask this question of yourself as a picker; am I making the defender and the defense's life meaningfully harder by setting this pick?
slides.subtitle: PICK

slides.text: Regardless, one of the functions of a pick is to clear space for ball carriers

OVERLAP - one of four basic motions that we want out players to leave this offense with a sense of; we need to help solve the perennial youth lacrosse problem of off ball players being in the way of ball carriers.
One player passes to another, and the ball carrier proceeds to carry, dodge back to where the ball came from.

Original player "overlaps" BEHIND the ball carrier and, like all the other motions, put his defender in a "decision position" and does not allow his defender (D11), to effectively cover two people at once
This creates space and a gap for the ball carrier to attack, and creates a moment of opportunity for the defense to make a mistake in their coverage. The offense could also execute a "flip" here.
slides.subtitle: OVERLAP
